speaker: Woman
en: Good afternoon, our beloved listeners.

speaker: Woman
en: Welcome to today's Capturing the Moment programme.

speaker: Woman
en: Today, we have Darren Almond here with us.

speaker: Woman
en: He's a college student who has just won an award for his set of shots of red squirrels.

speaker: Woman
en: Welcome, Darren.

speaker: Woman
en: Now, can you tell us a bit about this lovely creature and how you managed to take such wonderful photos?

speaker: Darren
en: Well, the red squirrel is Britain's only native squirrel.

speaker: Darren
en: It's graceful and athletic.

speaker: Darren
en: Its Latin name 'Sciurus Vulgaris' comes from the Greek words, describing a common type of animal which sits in the shadow of its own tail.

speaker: Darren
en: At first, I thought this description was funny and was a kind of exaggeration.

speaker: Darren
en: But now I realize that this name suits them very well.

speaker: Darren
en: Their fluffy tails under the sun normally cast a big shadow over themselves.

speaker: Darren
en: You will be lucky to see a red squirrel in the areas where they still survive, due to its timid nature and decreasing number in the wild.

speaker: Darren
en: In the late 1800s when grey squirrels, an exotic species, were first introduced into Britain, there were only about 20,000 red squirrels left, the majority of which lived in the north of England.

speaker: Darren
en: The greys carry a disease called the squirrelpox virus which could lead to the death of red squirrels.

speaker: Darren
en: But their number had already declined before the disease spread there.

speaker: Darren
en: The shrinking population actually corresponds to decreases in the number of nuts available, the red squirrels' primary food source.

speaker: Darren
en: I accidentally left some nuts in my garden one day.

speaker: Darren
en: And I found they had been eaten the next morning.

speaker: Darren
en: It could have been rats as they have a very similar food category to red squirrels.

speaker: Darren
en: But I knew it was red squirrels because of their different feeding habits.

speaker: Darren
en: Rats bite a hole in the nuts, whereas red squirrels crack the nuts in half and eat the kernels.

speaker: Darren
en: After a week or so, the red squirrels became regular visitors to my garden, making them ideal subjects for my photography project.

speaker: Darren
en: Unfortunately, I had to shoot only in my garden, partly because I couldn't find another spot to sight red squirrels on a regular basis.

speaker: Darren
en: Also, this could block out any outside distractions, and I could focus solely on my subject.

speaker: Darren
en: During the shooting, other animals like birds got caught in the frame together sometimes.

speaker: Darren
en: I'm more than satisfied with my photos.

speaker: Darren
en: Even though I only practiced a few times and used just a second-hand camera from my friend, my great mentor helped me with the shooting.

speaker: Darren
en: I got great tutorials from him and was able to shoot better photos.

speaker: Woman
en: Thank you, Darren.

speaker: Woman
en: The award-winning photos will be on display in a local museum for the coming month.

speaker: Woman
en: We are looking forward to the exhibition.

speaker: Woman
en: So, tell us about the award.

speaker: Darren
en: OK.

speaker: Darren
en: I sent my portfolio for the competition hosted by a national newspaper.

speaker: Darren
en: I wasn't expecting the third prize.

speaker: Darren
en: So, when they notified me of the result on the phone, I was really excited.

speaker: Woman
en: Do you have some tips for our audience to help improve their photography skills?

speaker: Darren
en: Of course.

speaker: Darren
en: Here are three things I think photographers can work on to get a better shot.

speaker: Darren
en: First of all, good composition is key to a lovely shot.

speaker: Darren
en: Normally, an image's centre of interest is placed at one of the intersections that trisect the whole picture.

speaker: Darren
en: In order to achieve balance, a 'Secondary Object', so to speak, can be added to the scene at the opposing intersection.

speaker: Darren
en: So, for instance, in one of my shots, a red squirrel at the top left is staring at a nut at the lower right corner.

speaker: Darren
en: This is called 'the Rule of Thirds'.

speaker: Darren
en: Lighting is another essential element if you want to be rewarded with a superb photo.

speaker: Darren
en: The direction of the light falling on your subject is most important of all.

speaker: Darren
en: You need to look at your subject carefully and watch how the shadows fall.

speaker: Darren
en: If you're able to choose the time of day to shoot your pictures, lighting is most ideal in the late afternoon.

speaker: Darren
en: Try to position yourself so that the sun hits your subject from the side.

speaker: Darren
en: This helps create a 3D effect in the picture.

speaker: Darren
en: To further improve on your photography skills, it is advised to take detailed notes of timing, position, weather, etc.

speaker: Darren
en: By doing so, it is easier to review and adjust accordingly, and there is always room for improvement.
